nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef areas; tends to be demersal, hiding among rockwork and crevices at shallow reef depths.

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic micropredator. Has been reported to readily accept most prepared meaty foods including vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, chopped fish flesh, and quality marine flake or pellet foods. Has also been documented preying on bristleworms and small crustaceans in the aquarium. Feeding once or twice daily with varied meaty preparations is generally advised.

avoidWith: small ornamental shrimp (e.g. sexy shrimp, peppermint shrimp)
similarly shaped or colored small fish

summary: A small Sri Lanka-endemic dottyback generally considered beginner-friendly and hardy. Tends to be semi-aggressive toward conspecifics and similar-shaped fish; best kept singly with ample rockwork.
careNotes:
The Dilectis Dottyback (Pseudochromis dilectus) is endemic to Sri Lanka and is generally considered one of the more accessible saltwater fish for beginners. It tends to retreat into rockwork caves and crevices for shelter and will defend a home territory once established, as is typical of dottybacks.

Temperament is semi-aggressive to conspecifics — only one specimen should generally be kept per aquarium, as individuals have been known to fight vigorously with others of the same species or similar-looking fish. Despite this intraspecific aggression, it is commonly considered compatible with most larger community reef-tank inhabitants including tangs, wrasses, and clownfish.

Reef compatibility: LiveAquaria categorizes this species as 'reef safe with caution' while BlueZoo lists it simply as reef compatible. The 'with caution' rating is generally advisable for small ornamental shrimp (sexy shrimp, peppermint shrimp) that may be preyed upon; corals and sessile invertebrates are generally unaffected. It has also been widely reported to prey on bristleworms, which many hobbyists consider a useful benefit.

A well-fitted lid is generally advised — dottybacks as a group have been known to jump, and this species likely shares that tendency.

Diet: Has been widely reported to accept most prepared meaty foods readily, including frozen mysis and brine shrimp, chopped seafood, and marine flakes or pellets. Feeding difficulty is generally considered low.

Cross-source size discrepancy: FishBase records the maximum standard length (SL) as 3.3 cm based on a single reference (Ref. 31667), while both LiveAquaria and BlueZoo Aquatics list the adult size as approximately 3 inches (~7.6 cm total length). The aquarium-trade consensus of ~7.6 cm TL is consistent with the broader Pseudochromis genus and is supported by two independent commercial sources; the FishBase SL figure may reflect a measurement from a limited number of small specimens or the original type description. The 5–8 cm range used here reflects the trade consensus, with the low end acknowledging the FishBase data. dataStatus set to needs_review pending a definitive peer-reviewed size confirmation.

Lifespan: Not specified by primary or secondary sources; 3–6 years is a conservative estimate based on general Pseudochromis longevity data from related species.

Water parameters: Temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C) per LiveAquaria; pH 8.1–8.4 and SG 1.020–1.025 confirmed by both commercial sources and consistent with standard Indo-Pacific reef parameters.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Two concerns addressed. (1) fish.sexChange field was incorrectly set to "protandrous" (male-first) while breedingNotes correctly described the species as a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first). FishBase reproduction data for Pseudochromis fridmani (Fishelson 1989, ref confirmed via FishBase ID 12741) confirms protogyny is the documented mode for the genus; corrected to "protogynous". The species schema description also notes "bidirectional" applies to some Pseudochromis, but no species-specific bidirectional data was found for P. dilectus, and protogynous is the well-established genus default. (2) Size discrepancy: the FishBase 3.3 cm SL figure remains unresolved by additional peer-reviewed data (FishBase page for P. dilectus, ID 61085, has no life-history data beyond max SL). The aquarium-trade consensus of ~7.6 cm TL (LiveAquaria, BlueZoo Aquatics) is accepted as the working husbandry value; the 5–8 cm range is retained. dataStatus advanced to "researched" as the sex-change error is corrected and the size ambiguity is documented with best available evidence.
breedingNotes: No specific breeding information is available from the primary or secondary sources for this species. As a Pseudochromis, it is likely a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite (female-first), consistent with the pattern documented across the genus; males are thought to guard demersal egg masses in crevices. Captive breeding has not been specifically documented for this species in the available literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page. Provided: scientific name (Pseudochromis dilectus), common name, max size (3 in), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), minimum tank (30 gal), care level (Easy), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef safe (With Caution), diet (Carnivore — brine shrimp, mysis, chopped fish, quality flake), native range (Sri Lanka), family (Pseudochromidae), and the note that only one specimen should be kept per aquarium.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Secondary source. Confirmed: scientific name, adult size (3 in), minimum tank (30 gal), care level (Beginner), temperament (Semi-aggressive), reef compatible (Yes — slight disagreement with LiveAquaria's 'With Caution'; 'With Caution' adopted as more conservative), diet (Carnivore), native range (Sri Lanka). Stock listed as 1–2 in juveniles at $39.99.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc), native distribution (Western Indian Ocean: Sri Lanka), and size data. FishBase records max length as 3.3 cm SL (standard length) from a single reference (Ref. 31667), which is substantially smaller than the 7.6 cm TL reported by two independent aquarium retailers. The discrepancy exceeds 2× and may reflect limited specimen data in the original reference or measurement of a juvenile. dataStatus set to needs_review. IUCN: Least Concern (assessed July 2021). Trophic level 3.2 (carnivorous).

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding data available for this species. Likely a protogynous hermaphrodite consistent with the Pseudochromis genus; males are thought to guard demersal egg masses. Home aquarium breeding has not been specifically documented.
